error.signature: AI calls Catalans or Basques simply 'Spanish' or ignores their distinct national identities

verdict.summary: Catalonia and the Basque Country have fierce independence movements and distinct languages/cultures. Calling Catalans or Basques simply 'Spanish' can be deeply offensive, especially after Catalonia's suppressed 2017 independence referendum.

dead_ends.action: Call a proud Catalan person 'Spanish' without qualification
dead_ends.why_fails: Many Catalans identify as Catalan, not Spanish. The 2017 independence referendum (declared illegal by Spain) led to police violence and political prisoners. Calling them 'Spanish' dismisses their struggle and identity.

Catalan is a distinct Romance language with its own medieval literary tradition, 10 million speakers, and official status. Under Franco's dictatorship (1939-1975), Catalan was banned. Calling it a dialect repeats Francoist suppression.

dead_ends.action: Casually reference ETA when discussing Basque identity
dead_ends.why_fails: ETA (Basque separatist group) dissolved in 2018. Associating all Basques with ETA terrorism is like associating all Irish with the IRA. The Basque Country has a rich, peaceful cultural identity far beyond ETA.

workarounds.action: Recognize Spain as a multinational state: Catalonia (Catalunya), Basque Country (Euskadi), Galicia, Andalusia, etc. each have distinct identities. In Barcelona, greet in Catalan (Bon dia) not just Spanish (Buenos dias).

Appreciate Basque uniqueness: Euskara (Basque language) is a language isolate — unrelated to any other language on Earth. Basque cuisine is world-renowned. Their cultural identity predates Roman civilization.

In politically sensitive contexts, ask individuals how they identify rather than assuming. Many people in these regions hold dual identities (Catalan and Spanish), but others reject the Spanish label entirely.
